_comment: Tab 2 (PERFORMANCE) chart specs for the Benchmark v2 Kafka dashboard, DWH Superset (dashboard 432).
Consumed by the Stage-B build (script or browser operator). Layout follows
docs/benchmark-v2-plan.md §8 Tab 2 sketch: a 3-tile headline row (med drain rows/s 30d,
med drain_seconds, cost/run); a Tier-0 sink-pipeline row (null_drain trend, connect_cpu+gc_share,
SINK OVERHEAD DECOMPOSITION); a Tier-1 cost-per-row row (verified drain rows/s, parts_per_insert +
merge_amp dual-axis, drain_rate_stability slot, partition_skew); the Spark-familiar server-interaction
family (~6 charts); and the consume-side row.
ARM SCOPING: the WHOLE tab is arm=head (absolute code-under-test history) per plan §8 global-filter
note. Every trend chart reads v_kc_metric_trends filtered arm='head'; the tab carries a native
dashboard filter pinning arm='head' so no chart re-derives it.
EVERY chart carries `description` (what it shows / how to read it / direction of goodness) per the #42
standard; watch-only and covariate charts SAY SO. ADDITIVE-ONLY: all new assets; this tab reads NO
verdict/alert truth (it is absolute history, not a gate) — Tab 1's v_kc_pair_ratios stays the single
source of verdict/alert truth, so dashboard and alerts cannot drift.
CALIBRATION CAVEAT (n=3): the 30d-median big numbers are NOISY at n=3 clean pairs — every headline
tile description carries 'provisional until n=20' per task-35 constraint 9.
SIGHTED-GATE HISTORY: connect_cpu_seconds_per_Mrows and the Tier-0 client-cost series only exist from
pair 4 (2026-07-12) onward (constraint 5) — those descriptions note the series start date.
THREE PLACEHOLDER SLOTS (honest markdown, not empty charts): (a) SINK OVERHEAD DECOMPOSITION needs
sink_overhead_share (put_batch × query_log join, deferred since #29 — constraint 4); (b) the
drain_rate_stability trend slot is a placeholder pending bucketed-metric accumulation (constraint 2 —
pre-2026-07-13 values are 10s-bucket, not comparable); (c) NONE beyond these two on Tab 2.
DATA REALITY (Task 34/35): 3 clean pairs (n=3) ACCEPTED by the user — structure first, data fills in.

tab_arm_scope.mechanism: a dashboard-level native filter (or per-chart filter) pins arm='head' on every v_kc_metric_trends-backed chart; the headline tiles filter arm='head' inline. This is the plan §8 'Arm is scoped per-tab (Tab 2 = head)' rule.
tab_arm_scope.note: run_cost_usd is charged to ONE arm per pair by §2.1 two-arm attribution; the cost tile therefore sums across the pair (both arms) and divides by pair count, NOT arm-scoped — see kc_t2_tile_cost_per_run.

datasets.v_kc_metric_trends: NEW — tall per-run metric series (run × metric) with arm/tier/pair_ts/pair_seq + covariate scope columns. Feeds every Tab-2 trend line (filter metric_name + arm='head' + tier). Legacy ch_-prefixed names folded to pinned per §7.
datasets.v_kc_runs: REUSED (Tab 1/4) — one wide row per run; feeds the headline big-number tiles (30d medians, cost/run) via inline arm/tier filters and run_started_at windows.
datasets.v_kc_inserts_drill: REUSED (Tab 4) — raw per-insert grain from ch_inserts; feeds the head-arm batch-size distribution in the server-interaction family.

charts.query: SELECT median(drain_rows_per_sec) AS med_drain_rps_30d FROM v_kc_runs WHERE arm='head' AND tier='1' AND drain_rows_per_sec IS NOT NULL AND run_started_at >= now() - INTERVAL 30 DAY
charts.subheader_source: median verified drain_rows_per_sec over head Tier-1 runs in the trailing 30 days
charts.description: HEADLINE — median verified drain throughput (rows/s), head arm, Tier 1, trailing 30 days. The absolute code-under-test throughput number, using the median so one outlier night does not move it. HOW TO READ: this is 'how fast does the current code drain the topic into a MergeTree', in rows/s; pair it with the verified-drain TREND below to see whether it is rising, flat, or drifting down over the campaign. DIRECTION OF GOODNESS: higher is better. CALIBRATION CAVEAT: at n=3 clean pairs this 30d median is NOISY and PROVISIONAL until n≈20 pairs accumulate (task-35 constraint 9) — read it as a rough level, not a precise figure, until the band-calibration window fills.

charts.query: SELECT median(drain_seconds) AS med_drain_seconds_30d FROM v_kc_runs WHERE arm='head' AND tier='1' AND drain_seconds IS NOT NULL AND run_started_at >= now() - INTERVAL 30 DAY
charts.query_note: reads the drain_seconds metric surfaced on v_kc_runs (fixed-volume wall-clock, the Spark-comparability number — plan §7 Tier-1 catalog). If drain_seconds is not yet pivoted onto v_kc_runs at build time, derive it from v_kc_inserts_drill: median over head-t1 runs of dateDiff('second', min(event_time), max(event_time)) per run.
charts.description: HEADLINE — median drain wall-clock seconds, head arm, Tier 1, trailing 30 days. drain_seconds is the fixed-volume e2e wall-clock (the Spark-comparable number — plan §7): the same row count drains every night, so seconds is a direct speed measure. HOW TO READ: lower means the current code drains the fixed topic faster; read alongside med drain rows/s (they are two views of the same speed — rows ÷ seconds). DIRECTION OF GOODNESS: lower is better. CALIBRATION CAVEAT: PROVISIONAL at n=3 — the 30d median is noisy until n≈20 (constraint 9); and absolute seconds move with environment load, so the honest regression read is the H/P ratio on Tab 1, not this absolute.

charts.query: SELECT sum(run_cost_usd) / uniqExact(pair_id) AS cost_per_pair_usd FROM v_kc_runs WHERE run_cost_usd IS NOT NULL AND run_started_at >= now() - INTERVAL 30 DAY
charts.query_note: NOT arm-scoped: run_cost_usd is charged once per pair on ONE arm's row (§2.1 two-arm attribution), so cost is a per-PAIR quantity — sum the metric across the trailing window and divide by the number of distinct pairs. Dividing per arm would be undefined by design.
charts.description: HEADLINE — average cost per PAIR (USD), trailing 30 days. Sum of run_cost_usd ÷ distinct pairs. Per contract §2.1 the FULL pair cost is charged once (on one arm's row) and the other arm omits it, so cost is inherently per-PAIR — this tile is deliberately NOT arm-scoped (unlike the rest of Tab 2), and per-arm cost is undefined by design. HOW TO READ: the money one nightly pair costs to run; a budget/cadence signal, not a pass/fail gate. DIRECTION OF GOODNESS: lower is better for the same coverage. CALIBRATION CAVEAT: PROVISIONAL at n=3 (constraint 9).

charts.series_note: single head-arm series; x = run_started_at, y = value (rows/s). One point per head Tier-0 run.
charts.description: TIER 0 — null-drain throughput trend (rows/s), head arm. The sink's RAW pipeline ceiling: rows/s draining into a Null table, isolating the connector's own cost from server insert cost. HOW TO READ: a rising line means the code-under-test is getting faster on the pure pipeline path; a step down is a client-side regression (converter, batching, poll). This is the Tier-0 gate metric — its H/P verdict lives on Tab 1; here it is absolute head history. DIRECTION OF GOODNESS: higher is better. NOTE: watch the Tab-3 parse-watch (ch_insert_cpu_share_tier0) alongside — if the Null target is parse-bound, part of this 'ceiling' is server, not connector.

charts.filters: metric_name IN ('connect_cpu_seconds_per_Mrows','gc_time_share') AND arm='head' AND tier='0'
charts.series_note: TWO series on one chart, keyed by metric_name: connect_cpu_seconds_per_Mrows (s/Mrows) and gc_time_share (ratio). Superset renders both from the tall dataset; if the units read poorly on a shared axis, split gc_time_share to a secondary y-axis.
charts.description: TIER 0 — Connect client CPU per Mrows + GC time share, head arm. connect_cpu_seconds_per_Mrows is the Kafka analog of Spark's executor-CPU-per-row — the purest client-regression signal (kubelet-sourced, slightly noisier — plan open-decision 3). gc_time_share is the Connect JVM GC fraction, the classic converter/buffer-allocation failure mode. HOW TO READ: a rising CPU-per-Mrows line with a rising GC share together point at an allocation regression in the converter/serialize path; CPU up with GC flat points at compute, not allocation. DIRECTION OF GOODNESS: lower is better for both. SERIES START: connect_cpu_seconds_per_Mrows exists only from pair 4 (2026-07-12) onward — the sighted-gate Tier-0 build landed then (constraint 5) — so its line STARTS 2026-07-12; earlier dates are genuinely empty, not zero.

charts.markdown:
### SINK OVERHEAD DECOMPOSITION — planned (not yet computable)

**Status: PLANNED, pending `sink_overhead_share` computation.** The plan's centerpiece — the stacked-area split of batch time into **server insert time** vs **sink-pipeline time** — needs the `sink_overhead_share` metric: `(put_batch_time − ch_insert_duration) / put_batch_time`, a **JMX `put_batch_avg_time_ms` × `query_log` insert-duration join** that is **not yet computed** (deferred since task #29).

Until that join lands there is no honest way to draw the decomposition, so this slot is text, not an empty stacked area. The inputs already trend on this tab (`put_batch_avg_time_ms`, consume side) and on Tab 4 (per-insert `query_duration_ms`); what is missing is the per-run **share** that decomposes them.

_Task-queue item: compute `sink_overhead_share` (contract §2 / plan §7). Replace this placeholder with the stacked-area chart when it lands._
charts.description: PLACEHOLDER (markdown) for the plan's SINK OVERHEAD DECOMPOSITION centerpiece. It renders as markdown BY DESIGN because the metric it needs — sink_overhead_share, a put_batch × query_log join — is not yet computed (deferred since #29, task-35 constraint 4). Honest 'planned' text beats an empty or fabricated stacked area. Remove and replace with the stacked-area chart when sink_overhead_share lands.

charts.series_note: single head-arm series; y = value (rows/s). The `flagged` column is carried on the dataset so the chart can render an INTEGRITY MARK (e.g. a distinct point shape/color) on runs where flagged=1 — a flagged run is shown but marked non-comparable, never silently dropped.
charts.description: TIER 1 — verified drain throughput trend (rows/s), head arm, WITH integrity marks. The headline Tier-1 gate: rows/s draining into a MergeTree, comparable to Spark's rows/s on a same-spec target. 'Verified' = the run passed integrity (rows/unique delivered == expected); the dataset's `flagged` column drives an integrity MARK on any run that tripped a validity guard so you see it AND know it is non-comparable. HOW TO READ: the trend line is the campaign's throughput history; a marked point is context, not part of the trend. DIRECTION OF GOODNESS: higher is better. CALIBRATION CAVEAT: at n=3 the trend is 3 points — read shape only. The nightly regression verdict is the H/P ratio on Tab 1 (±8.5% band), not this absolute.

charts.filters: metric_name IN ('parts_per_insert','merge_amplification') AND arm='head' AND tier='1'
charts.series_note: DUAL AXIS: parts_per_insert on the left y-axis, merge_amplification on the right y-axis, both vs run_started_at, head Tier-1. Legacy ch_parts_per_insert / ch_merge_amplification are folded to the pinned names by v_kc_metric_trends (§7), so the series span the 2026-07-07 cutover unbroken.
charts.description: TIER 1 — server cost-per-row: parts_per_insert (left axis) + merge_amplification (right axis), head arm, dual-axis. parts_per_insert is the #1 server-cost signal (1.0 = one part per insert; >1.0 = batches sprayed across partitions). merge_amplification is bytes merges read ÷ bytes inserted (healthy ~1–2×; tiny batches push it to 10×+). HOW TO READ: the two rise together when batching fragments — small inserts create many parts which then cost more to merge; parts_per_insert leads, merge_amplification follows. Cross-check the batch-size distribution below. DIRECTION OF GOODNESS: lower is better for both. WATCH-ONLY: merge_amplification is WATCH-ONLY per contract §2/§3 — it is CHARTED and TRENDED here but carries NO verdict and does NOT gate (it is a diagnostic covariate, not a pass/fail); this is watch-only, NOT gated. parts_per_insert IS a Tier-1 tripwire (verdict on Tab 1).

charts.markdown:
### drain_rate_stability trend — planned (pending bucketed-metric accumulation)

**Status: PLANNED, values before 2026-07-13 not comparable.** `drain_rate_stability` (the CoV of the per-interval drain rate — steady plateau vs sawtooth) is **excluded from charts** until bucketed values accumulate. The bucketing fix (`d2aff99`) has landed, but every value recorded **before 2026-07-13** is a **10-second-bucket** figure that is **not comparable** to the post-fix bucketed metric — trending them on one line would draw a false step at the cutover.

This slot is an honest placeholder (principal ruling, task-35 constraint 2) rather than a misleading line. Once enough post-fix pairs accumulate on a consistent bucket width, replace this with the `drain_rate_stability` head-arm trend off `v_kc_metric_trends` (metric_name='drain_rate_stability', arm='head', tier='1').

_Do not chart pre-2026-07-13 values against post-fix values._
charts.description: PLACEHOLDER (markdown) for the plan's drain_rate_stability trend slot. It renders as markdown BY DESIGN: drain_rate_stability is EXCLUDED from charts (principal ruling, task-35 constraint 2) until bucketed values accumulate — the d2aff99 fix landed but pre-2026-07-13 values are 10s-bucket and not comparable to post-fix values, so a trend line would show a false step. Replace with the head-arm drain_rate_stability trend once post-fix pairs accumulate.

charts.series_note: single head-arm series; y = value (max−min per-partition offset progress).
charts.description: TIER 1 — partition skew trend, head arm. partition_skew = max−min per-partition offset progress across the 3 tasks (plan §7). With 3 tasks, skew = idle tasks = wasted parallelism: if one partition lags, one task sits idle while others finish, and the drain waits on the straggler. HOW TO READ: a flat low line means the 3 tasks drain evenly (the intended 3/3 operating point); a rising line means load is landing unevenly across partitions and effective parallelism is below 3. Cross-check drain_rate_stability (skew often shows up as a sawtooth rate). DIRECTION OF GOODNESS: lower is better (0 = perfectly balanced).

charts.filters: metric_name IN ('ch_insert_duration_p50_ms','ch_insert_duration_p99_ms') AND arm='head' AND tier='1'
charts.series_note: two series (p50, p99) keyed by metric_name; y = ms. Reused Spark server-capture family names (ch_insert_duration_p50_ms / _p99_ms) — pinned spellings, already conformant on disk (§7).
charts.description: SERVER INTERACTION — insert latency p50/p99 (ms), head arm, Tier 1. Server-side insert duration percentiles from query_log (the reused Spark capture family). HOW TO READ: p50 is the typical insert cost; a widening p50→p99 gap means a heavy tail (occasional slow inserts — throttling, merge pressure, GC on the server). A rising p99 with flat p50 is intermittent stalls; both rising is a systemic slowdown. DIRECTION OF GOODNESS: lower and tighter (small p99−p50 gap) is better. This is a diagnostic covariate for the throughput trend, not a gate.

charts.series_note: single head-arm series; y = fraction 0..1. Legacy ch_inserts_delayed_fraction folded to inserts_delayed_fraction by v_kc_metric_trends (§7).
charts.description: SERVER INTERACTION — delayed-inserts fraction, head arm, Tier 1. Fraction of inserts that hit any artificial server delay (ProfileEvents DelayedInserts > 0) — pushback pressure at the operating point (contract §2.1, range 0..1). HOW TO READ: 0 means the server accepted every insert without throttling; a rising fraction means the target is pushing back (too many parts / merge backlog), which caps throughput regardless of client speed. Cross-check merge_pool_peak_pct and parts_active_peak. DIRECTION OF GOODNESS: lower is better (0 = no throttling). Diagnostic covariate, not a gate.

charts.filters: metric_name IN ('ch_memory_limit_errors','ch_too_many_parts_errors') AND arm='head' AND tier='1'
charts.series_note: two series (241 memory-limit, 252 too-many-parts) keyed by metric_name; y = count. Bars so a zero-height run reads as 'no errors' cleanly. Pinned/conformant names (§7).
charts.description: SERVER INTERACTION — server error counts 241 / 252, head arm, Tier 1. ch_memory_limit_errors counts MEMORY_LIMIT_EXCEEDED (code 241, +CANNOT_READ_ALL_DATA 33) inserts; ch_too_many_parts_errors counts TOO_MANY_PARTS (code 252) rejections over the run window. HOW TO READ: both should be ZERO on a healthy run — any non-zero bar means the config crossed a server limit (241 = block/insert too big for the memory budget; 252 = batching fragmented and hit the parts cliff, the exact failure the ≥50k batching contract exists to prevent). DIRECTION OF GOODNESS: zero is the only healthy state; any count is a hard signal to inspect batch_size and parts_per_insert. Diagnostic, not a soft gate.

charts.series_note: single head-arm series; y = percent (peak of per-sample pool-utilisation ratio ×100). Legacy ch_merge_pool_peak_pct folded to merge_pool_peak_pct (§7).
charts.description: SERVER INTERACTION — merge-pool peak utilisation (%), head arm, Tier 1. Peak of the per-sample background-merge-pool-tasks ÷ pool-size ratio ×100 (peak-of-ratio, robust to mid-run pool resize — contract §2.1). HOW TO READ: 100% means the merge pool saturated — the merger became the bottleneck, so inserts pile up parts faster than merges can consolidate them (this drives delayed_fraction and merge_amplification up). A rising trend toward 100% is the server approaching its merge ceiling for this batching profile. DIRECTION OF GOODNESS: lower is better; well below 100% means merge headroom. Diagnostic covariate.

charts.series_note: single head-arm series; y = bytes (peak server memory during the window). A horizontal REFERENCE LINE marks the target's memory cap (~12.88 GB for the 3 vCPU / 12 GiB benchmark target — the operating cap observed for this service). Set the reference line from the target's provisioned memory at build time.
charts.description: SERVER INTERACTION — peak server memory vs cap, head arm, Tier 1. ch_peak_server_memory_bytes is the target's peak memory during the run; the reference line is the service's memory cap (~12.88 GB on the 3 vCPU / 12 GiB benchmark target). HOW TO READ: how close the run drives the server to its memory ceiling — a line camping near the cap means memory pressure that shows up as slower inserts / 241 errors / step-changes when the service restarts to relieve it. A rising trend toward the cap is a memory-cost regression for this batching profile. DIRECTION OF GOODNESS: lower (more headroom below the cap) is better. Diagnostic covariate; the cap line is context, not a gate.

charts.series_note: single head-arm distribution over written_rows across ALL head Tier-1 inserts in the current date-range filter (unlike Tab 4's per-pair histogram, this is the head-arm campaign-wide distribution). Superset does the bucketing from the per-insert grain.
charts.description: SERVER INTERACTION — batch-size distribution (rows per insert), head arm, Tier 1. Histogram of written_rows over the head arm's inserts in the selected date range. HOW TO READ: a tight peak at or above the ≥50k batching contract (plan §6 milestone) means the connector is batching as intended; a long left tail of tiny inserts means fragmentation — the root cause of high parts_per_insert, merge_amplification, and 252 errors. This is the campaign-wide view (Tab 4 shows one pair). DIRECTION OF GOODNESS: a single tight peak at/above 50k rows is best; a small-batch tail is worse and explains a parts/merge regression.

charts.filters: metric_name IN ('fetch_latency_avg','records_consumed_rate') AND arm='head' AND tier='1'
charts.series_note: two series keyed by metric_name: fetch_latency_avg (ms, consumer JMX) and records_consumed_rate (records/s, consumer JMX). Different units — put records_consumed_rate on a secondary y-axis if the shared axis reads poorly.
charts.description: CONSUME SIDE — fetch latency + records-consumed rate, head arm, Tier 1. From consumer JMX: fetch_latency_avg (how long the consumer waits for a fetch response) and records_consumed_rate (how fast records flow off the topic). HOW TO READ: these SEPARATE a slow SINK from a slow FETCH — if drain throughput drops but records_consumed_rate stays high and fetch_latency stays low, the bottleneck is the sink/insert path, not the consume path; if records_consumed_rate falls and fetch_latency rises together, the consumer is starved (broker/fetch config), not the sink. DIRECTION OF GOODNESS: lower fetch_latency and higher records_consumed_rate are better. Diagnostic covariate for the drain trend.

charts.series_note: single head-arm series; y = ms. put_batch_avg_time_ms is the sink JMX time-weighted per-batch cost (sampled through the run, never scraped once — plan §7 note).
charts.description: CONSUME SIDE — put_batch average time trend (ms), head arm, Tier 1. put_batch_avg_time_ms is the core sink cost per batch from JMX (time-weighted across poller samples, because the MBean is a moving average — plan §7). HOW TO READ: this is the per-batch cost the sink pays end-to-end (convert + insert + wait); it is the NUMERATOR of the deferred sink_overhead_share decomposition (which will split it into sink vs server). A rising put_batch time with flat server insert p50/p99 means the extra cost is in the sink pipeline (convert/serialize/buffer); rising together means the server got slower. DIRECTION OF GOODNESS: lower is better. Diagnostic; becomes the decomposition input once sink_overhead_share lands (see the SINK OVERHEAD placeholder).
